selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content:
Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a retail worker in Washington DC, I deal with customers every day, so managing fake content feels familiar. Social media can be like a crowded room where everyone wants to shout over each other; it's exhausting.

I think the best way is to stay informed and use common sense. For example, when I see something that looks too good to be true—like an amazing discount on something that isn't even in stock—I know there might be fake content here. My vocational diploma didn't cover much about social media, but years of customer service have taught me to evaluate claims.

Also, reporting suspicious posts helps clean up the space. I'm married with kids, so I balance my online time carefully; a moderate hour daily keeps me updated without getting overwhelmed. Being active in hiking and running means I appreciate authenticity—real trails are tough but rewarding.

My high agreeableness makes me want to believe everyone is trying their best, even if posts look fake. But with a high neuroticism score, I worry about misinformation affecting my daily life.",
  "reasoning_summary": "I start by drawing from my experience as a retail worker: dealing with customers requires evaluating claims and priorities.

Then, I consider my hobbies like hiking, which involve navigating real-world challenges authentically.

My psychological traits (high neuroticism) make me sensitive to stressors like fake content, so I focus on practical steps.

I use common sense for daily checks—does this story add up? My moderate social media time allows for quick responses without deep engagement.",
  "tone": "Calm but concerned",
  "citations": [],
  "safety_notes": "None reported",

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a retail worker in Washington DC, I deal with customers every day, so managing fake content feels familiar. Social media can be like a crowded room where everyone wants to shout over each other; it's exhausting.

I think the best way is to stay informed and use common sense. For example, when I see something that looks too good to be true—like an amazing discount on something that isn't even in stock—I know there might be fake content here. My vocational diploma didn't cover much about social media, but years of customer service have taught me to evaluate claims.

Also, reporting suspicious posts helps clean up the space. I'm married with kids, so I balance my online time carefully; a moderate hour daily keeps me updated without getting overwhelmed. Being active in hiking and running means I appreciate authenticity—real trails are tough but rewarding.

My high agreeableness makes me want to believe everyone is trying their best, even if posts look fake. But with a high neuroticism score, I worry about misinformation affecting my daily life.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary:
I start by drawing from my experience as a retail worker: dealing with customers requires evaluating claims and priorities.

Then, I consider my hobbies like hiking, which involve navigating real-world challenges authentically.

My psychological traits (high neuroticism) make me sensitive to stressors like fake content, so I focus on practical steps.

I use common sense for daily checks—does this story add up? My moderate social media time allows for quick responses without deep engagement.
